title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for a library digital-help project. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Nora, The Appointment Coordinator
Nora joined the library’s digital help project after watching a neighbour struggle to book a medical appointment online. She thought she would simply sit beside people at computers, but the waiting list quickly became her responsibility. [47]The first difficulty was getting the computer room unlocked early enough, because the key was kept at the main desk, which opened after several elderly learners arrived.[/47] Nobody liked beginning a lesson by standing in a corridor. [48]Her experience as a dental receptionist helped her organise appointments, reminder calls and notes about each learner’s goals without confusing private information.[/48] She also learned to leave extra time for people who needed to practise slowly. Nora says the project changed her view of technology. A password reset may look small to outsiders, but to someone who has felt excluded for years, it can feel like getting a door back. That lesson has stayed with her. daily.
texts.B:
### Sam, The Online Safety Tutor
[49]Sam was asked to volunteer after the librarian heard him explaining phone scams clearly to his mother in the reading room.[/49] He had worked in customer support, but teaching face to face felt different. [50]He was surprised by the consent forms and safeguarding rules required before volunteers could help learners open email accounts or discuss personal messages.[/50] At first, he feared the paperwork would make people suspicious. Instead, it made boundaries clear and helped learners decide what they wanted to share. Sam’s most memorable session involved a retired builder who learned to spot a fake delivery text. The man returned the following week with three examples he had saved for discussion. Sam says online safety is not about frightening people. It is about giving them enough confidence to pause, check and ask one sensible question before clicking. He now keeps examples simple, local and close to real life. every week.
texts.C:
### Imogen, The Poster Volunteer
Imogen, a marketing assistant, became involved after waiting in the library every Thursday while her daughter attended chess club. [51]During those waits, she noticed people walking past the digital help table because the sign looked like a notice for staff training.[/51] She offered to redesign the posters and desk cards. [55]Her materials used friendly questions and simple icons so visitors could quickly see that help was available for phones, forms, passwords and video calls.[/55] [52]The most disappointing moment came when the first posters were pinned behind a display stand where almost nobody could see them.[/52] Imogen felt her effort had been wasted, but the librarian apologised and moved them to the entrance. She says the experience reminded her that good information has to be placed where anxious people are already looking. A clever message hidden in the wrong corner helps no one at all. Visibility became her most important rule.
texts.D:
### Victor, The Home Visit Link
[53]Victor volunteered because his aunt could no longer travel to the library but still needed help using her tablet to contact family abroad.[/53] He began arranging home visits for people with mobility problems. The role required more sensitivity than he expected, because volunteers entered private spaces and saw how isolated some learners had become. [54]His hardest task was scheduling visits so helpers arrived quietly, respected carers’ routines and did not make residents feel inspected or rushed.[/54] He learned to ask before touching any device, even when the solution seemed obvious. Victor was moved when a man made his first video call to a grandson overseas. [56]He is now writing visit guidelines so the service can continue safely without relying on his personal judgement for every appointment.[/56] He says digital confidence is often built in someone’s kitchen, one careful step at a time. That patient pace matters more than speed. always.

questions.question: mentions learners having to wait because the room key was not available early enough?
questions.explanation: Nora says the computer room key was kept at the main desk, which opened later than learners arrived.

questions.question: used experience from reception work to organise appointments and learner notes?
questions.explanation: Nora’s dental receptionist experience helped her organise appointments, reminder calls and notes.

questions.question: was recruited after being overheard explaining a related problem clearly?
questions.explanation: Sam was asked to volunteer after the librarian heard him explaining phone scams.

questions.question: was surprised by rules around helping people with private online information?
questions.explanation: Sam was surprised by consent forms and safeguarding rules for email accounts and personal messages.

questions.question: noticed people were ignoring the service because the sign gave the wrong impression?
questions.explanation: Imogen noticed people walking past because the sign looked like staff training.

questions.question: was disappointed when finished promotional material was placed where people could not see it?
questions.explanation: Imogen’s first posters were pinned behind a display stand.

questions.question: became involved because a relative needed help using technology from home?
questions.explanation: Victor’s aunt could no longer travel to the library but needed help with her tablet.

questions.question: had to arrange visits respectfully around people’s home routines?
questions.explanation: Victor scheduled visits so helpers respected carers’ routines and did not rush residents.

questions.question: designed materials showing quickly what kinds of digital help were available?
questions.explanation: Imogen made materials showing help for phones, forms, passwords and video calls.

questions.question: is writing guidelines so the service can continue safely without relying on their judgement?
questions.explanation: Victor is writing visit guidelines for future volunteers.
